coredata.dc:description: Recent studies have shown that human oncoviruses are responsible for 10% of cancer cases worldwide. While PCR assays are highly sensitive and specific, microarrays offer outstanding performance for detecting multiple targets simultaneously. However, in microarray systems, accurate detection of DNA viruses relies on the type of microarray platform, while obtaining high-quality, fluorescent-labelled single-stranded DNA bares an equal contribution. Our study demonstrates a proof-of-concept of a microarray platform for enhanced DNA detection. First, we assembled 3D microarray chips on silicon nanowires (SiNWs) substrates using a one-step metal-assisted chemical etching (1-MACE) technique, coated them with SU-8 polymer, and ornated with carbon quantum dots (CDs) in either poly(diallyldimethylammonium) PDDA or poly(ethyleneimine) PEI solutions. Second, we obtained high-quality labelled HPV 16 target ssDNA through classic PCR and asymmetric PCR with a nested primer labelled with Cy5, then hybridised the ssDNA on the SiNWs/SU-8/CDs platforms. The fluorescent signal of hybridised DNA was strongest on the SiNWs/SU-8 substrates, and adding CDs further enhanced the signal. The best signal intensities (4.303, 3.7) and coefficients of variation (3.12%, 2.59%) were achieved by combining HPV 16 probes co-immobilised with CDs functionalised with PDDA. Our study conclusively demonstrates the vast potential of this microarray platform for improved DNA detection.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Fabrication and characterisation workflow of a microarray platform based on SiNWs/CDs heterostructures (Image created with BioRender).
Layout of the arrays printed on nanostructured Si.
SEM images of (a) SiNWs, (b) SiNWs/SU-8, (c) SiNWs/SU-8/CDs-PDDA, (d) SiNWs/SU-8/CDs-PEI.
HR-TEM images of CDs deposited on SiNWs/ SU-8 by (a) PDDA and (b) PEI.
Dynamic light scattering and zeta potential measurements of CDs-PDDA and CDs-PEI.
UV-Raman spectra of the SiNWs, SiNWs/SU-8 and CDs-PDDA, CDs-PEI samples.
Deconvoluted UV-Raman spectra of the SiNWs, SiNWs/SU-8 and CDs-PDDA/PEI samples.
C1s spectra for (a) SiNWs, (b) SiNWs/SU-8, (c) SiNWs/SU-8/ssDNA, (d) SiNWs/SU-8/dsDNA, (e) SiNWs/SU-8/ssDNA/PDDA, (f) SiNWs/SU-8/ssDNA/PEI, (g) SiNWs/SU-8/dsDNA/PDDA and (h) SiNWs/SU-8/dsDNA/PEI.
Overlayed N1s spectra for samples containing (a) ssDNA and for (b) ds DNA.
Photoluminescence spectra of carbon dots and Cy5.
Hybridisation results obtained on SiNWs with (a) unpurified reaction product and (b) purified reaction product.
Graphical analysis of the hybridisation results obtained on SiNWs with (a) unpurified reaction product and (b) the purified reaction product.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Recent studies have shown that human oncoviruses are responsible for 10% of cancer cases worldwide. While PCR assays are highly sensitive and specific, microarrays offer outstanding performance for detecting multiple targets simultaneously. However, in microarray systems, accurate detection of DNA viruses relies on the type of microarray platform, while obtaining high-quality, fluorescent-labelled single-stranded DNA bares an equal contribution.

Our study demonstrates a proof-of-concept of a microarray platform for enhanced DNA detection. First, we assembled 3D microarray chips on silicon nanowires (SiNWs) substrates using a one-step metal-assisted chemical etching (1-MACE) technique, coated them with SU-8 polymer, and ornated with carbon quantum dots (CDs) in either poly(diallyldimethylammonium) PDDA or poly(ethyleneimine) PEI solutions. Second, we obtained high-quality labelled HPV 16 target ssDNA through classic PCR and asymmetric PCR with a nested primer labelled with Cy5, then hybridised the ssDNA on the SiNWs/SU-8/CDs platforms.

The fluorescent signal of hybridised DNA was strongest on the SiNWs/SU-8 substrates, and adding CDs further enhanced the signal. The best signal intensities (4.303, 3.7) and coefficients of variation (3.12%, 2.59%) were achieved by combining HPV 16 probes co-immobilised with CDs functionalised with PDDA. Our study conclusively demonstrates the vast potential of this microarray platform for improved DNA detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Human oncoviruses - Epstein-Barr virus (EBV), human T-lymphotropic virus 1, hepatitis B virus, hepatitis C virus, HIV, human herpesvirus 8 (HHV-8), and human papillomavirus (HPV) - are known to play a role in tumorigenesis, with ∼1.4 million cancer cases annually, translated by 10% of the cancer burden worldwide [1,2]. Cervical cancer is the second leading reason of death among women, caused by the persistent infection and chromosomal integration of HPV types with high oncogenic potential [3]. Cytology-based screening (Pap test) is followed by HPV genotyping typically based on real-time PCR (i.e., Cobas 4800) [4] for assessing the cancer risk in women with abnormal cervical results.

DNA microarray biochips [5–9] have emerged as an alternative to multiplex real-time PCR for genotyping applications While PCR assays offer higher specificity and sensitivity, microarrays excel in simultaneous multi-targeting [10]. However, the performance of conventional microarray systems is often hindered by their reliance on bi-dimensional substrates [11,12]. To address this limitation, researchers have explored nanostructured silicon-based microarray systems, which offer several advantages such as a three-dimensional configuration [15,16], photoluminescence [17–19], and low reflectance with absorption in the visible spectrum [13]. Although innovative biosensors based on silicon nanowires (SiNWs) have been reported [19–24], their full potential in optical biosensors, particularly microarrays, remains largely untapped.

The efficacy of microarray biosensors also relies on the fluorescent detection of hybridised DNA, necessitating the preparation of high-quality fluorescently labelled single-stranded DNA (ssDNA). The generation of ssDNA, instrumental for biological applications [25,26], can be achieved by numerous methods [26,27], including straightforward strategies such as asymmetric PCR (aPCR), which uses an unequal molar ratio of forward and reverse primers for the preferential amplification of a single strand [28–31]. Two-step aPCR has been proven helpful for integrated PCR and microarray in microfluidic systems [32], detection of miRNA on silicon photonic sensor arrays [33], SPR detection [34], or selection of highly specific aptamers using SELEX [35,36]. In addition to achieving reliable DNA sample labelling, various strategies for fluorescence enhancement in biosensing applications have been explored [37–39]. Among the optical materials investigated for their favourable properties, carbon dots (CDs) are zero-dimensional (0D) optical materials that received consideration due to their facile preparation, tuneable fluorescent emission and applicability in optoelectronics, biosensors and bio-imaging [40–42]. They proved suitable in fast bacteria gram-type identification [37], and the enhanced detection of viral genetic material [43] and miRNA [39].

In this study, we aimed to develop a proof-of-concept of a microarray platform for enhanced DNA detection. Our approach involved the fabrication of heterostructures using SiNWs obtained by one-step metal-assisted chemical etching (1-MACE). The SiNWs were then coated with SU-8 and locally decorated with CDs and single-stranded DNA probes. Our team prepared two types of carbon dots in a one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method, one functionalised with poly(ethyleneimine) – CDs-PEI – and the other functionalised with poly(diallyldimethylammonium) – CDs-PDDA. The fluorescence enhancement provided by the existence of CDs was explored with the target DNA fluorescently labelled in a two-step asymmetric PCR (aPCR) reaction (Fig. 1 ).

To achieve our objectives, we implemented a conformal fabrication process to create SiNWs with a high aspect ratio, providing a significant active area for the microarray experiments. The SU-8 coating enabled the covalent bonding of carbon dots and DNA probes. The CDs functionalised with PDDA and PEI favoured increased fluorescence of the Cy5 dye attached to the ssDNA generated by asymmetric PCR, leading to an enhanced signal-to-noise ratio of the biochip. We revealed that the best DNA hybridisation signal intensities were obtained using unpurified PCR amplicons with the following succession in fluorescent intensities: CDs-PDDA (4.303) > CDs-PEI (3.959) > no CDs (3.744). To our knowledge, this is the first time CDs were used for signal enhancement in microarray biochips.

The p-type silicon wafers were ordered from SIEGERT WAFER (Germany). The oligonucleotides were provided by Biomers.net (Germany) and EurofinsGenomics (Eurofins Scientific, Germany). The OptiTaq PCR Master Mix (2x) Kit (EURx, Poland) was used for all PCR reactions. Agarose (Solarbio Life Sciences, China) and 1x TAE buffer were used for horizontal electrophoresis. The gel was stained with SimplySafe (EURx. Poland) and DNA amplicons mixed with loading dye (Biotium, California). 100 bp DNA Ladder (Simply) was used as a molecular marker during electrophoresis. SU-8 2002 photoresist was acquired from Microchem (USA). Nuclease-free water and coverslips were purchased from Roth (Germany). DNA hybridisation buffer was obtained from Sigma-Aldrich (Germany). Unless otherwise stated, Sigma-Aldrich (Germany) supplied all the other chemicals.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Etching of silicon nanostructures and functionalisation
ce:section.ce:para: The silicon nanowires (SiNWs) were obtained by one-step metal-assisted chemical etching (1-MACE), well-established in our previous reports [14,16]. In short, p-type Si substrates with 1–5 Ω cm were immersed in 0.06 M AgNO3 and 4.5 M HF, for 40 min in a dark chamber. Afterward, the chips were dipped into 60% v/v HNO3 solution, for 30 min, to remove any residual catalyst and, immersed in 5% v/v HF solution for 1 min.
The nano substrate was coated with SU-8 2002 photoresist by spinning at 4000 rpm, soft baking for 1 min at 65 °C, and another 2 min at 95 °C session, followed by post-baking at 180 °C for 30 min.

Amine-functionalised carbon dots (CDs-PEI) [44] were synthesised in a one-step microwave-assisted hydrothermal method using a microwave acid digestion vessel (4781 type, a PTFE cup from Parr Instrument Company) and a commercial microwave oven. Briefly, poly(ethyleneimine) (1.35 g), D-(+)-glucosamine hydrochloride (2.30 g), and deionised water (3.3 mL) were immersed in an ultrasound bath (5 min@25 kHz) to promote homogenisation of the reaction mixture. The reaction vial was kept at 40 °C for 1 h, and the solution was further processed (7 s, @700 W). Ultimately the reaction vessel was cooled down at 4 °C for 30 min, the resulting mixture being centrifuged (8500 rpm, 10 min, room temperature), collecting the supernatant and using it for ensuing experiments.

Poly(diallyldimethylammonium chloride) (15 mL. 20% wt. solution in water) and D-(+)-glucose were mixed, and heated, for 5 min at 700 W, using a PTFE cup in a conventional microwave oven in order to obtain poly(diallyldimethylammonium-functionalised graphene quantum dots (CDs-PDDA). After cooling down the reaction mixture (4 °C, 30 min), the centrifuged supernatant (8500 rpm, 10 min, room temperature) was collected and used for further experiments.

The morpho-structural characterisation of the uncoated SiNWs and coated with SU-8 was conducted by Electron Microscopy employing Field Emission - Scanning Electron Microscope (FE-SEM) Nova NanoSEM 630 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), Environmental-SEM Quattro S ESEM (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA), and High-Resolution Scanning/Transmission Electron Microscope (HR-S/TEM) Titan Themis 200 (Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) equipped with Super-X System for Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDS). Nova NanoSEM 630 was employed to highlight the morphology of the nanostructured microarray substrates by collecting the secondary electrons (SE) with the Through-the-Lens Detector (TLD), at 20kx magnification in high-vacuum mode. Low accelerating energies (high voltage of 5 kV and probe current of 56 pA) were used to mitigate the charging effects of the SiNWs and SU-8 coating. SEM images were thoroughly acquired in-plane view, at 60° tilting, and in cross-section, for comparative examination of SiNWs, SiNWs/SU-8, SiNWs/SU-8/CDs-PDDA, and SiNWs/SU-8/CDs-PEI. The size of the CDs is on par with the silver nanoparticles (Ag NPs) from the AgNO3 solution. High-resolution SEM (HR-SEM) images were collected in Immerssion mode at 12 kV and 300kX magnification on regions of every sample that were slightly scratched with a fine point and tilted at 5° for a thorough examination of the residual Ag NPs. To make a clear distinction between the Ag NPs and CDs, Quattro S ESEM was further employed to acquire pure-BSE images by using all four concentric segments at once (CBS mode) of the retractable DBS detector, whilst the SE images were obtained with the Everhart-Thornley Detector (ETD) at 30 kV, spot 3.5, and 80kx magnification. Bright Field TEM (BF-TEM) imaging disclosed the Ag NPs and allowed the elemental labelling of Ag in our structures by EDS technique. High-resolution TEM (HR-TEM) images of CDs were obtained at 4 k × 4 k pixels by using the microscope in the transmission mode at a voltage of 200 kV [45] and accurately labelled in Gatan Microscopy Suite GMS (Gatan Ametek, SUA) by the measurements of the space between lattices [46]. For the TEM sample preparation, small amounts of material were scraped off from the SiNWs/SU-8/CDs-PDDA and -PEI chips, then diluted into ethanol and placed onto a 400 mesh holey carbon-coated film copper grid [47].

The UV-Raman spectra were collected by LabRam HR800 spectrometer (Horiba Jobin Yvon GmbH, Germany), equipped with a 325 nm laser line, a grating of 2400 lines and lines, and a 40×/0.47 NUV objective (sample spot size less than 1 μm). The edge filter prevented us from recording UV-Raman spectra at low wavenumbers. Silicon was used for peak position calibration. The PeakFit 4.12 software was used for fitting of the background corrected spectra.

The X-ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) analysis was achieved using a K-Alpha instrument from Thermo Scientific (USA), equipped with a monochromated Al Kα source (1486.6 eV) at a bass pressure of 2 × 10–––9 mbar. Charging effects were compensated by using an Ar ion-based flood gun. The binding energies were acquired with 200 eV pass energy for the survey spectra and 20 eV for the high-resolution spectra. Internal calibration was done by placing the C 1 s peak at 284.8 eV considered as internal standard. Deconvolution of high-resolution C1s and N1s spectra was realized using Smart background, and secondary peaks were calculated through the Powel algorithm with convolved Gaussian-Lorentzian ratio by maintaining the values for Chi-Square at the lowest value as well as Abbe criterion minimization.
Photoluminescence emission measurements were performed on the fluorescence spectrometer FLS920 (Edinburgh Instruments Ltd, UK) equipped with a 450 W xenon lamp as an excitation source.

The hydrodynamic diameters and surface charges of CDs were performed with DelsaTMNano C (Beckman Coulter, USA) particle analyser by means of dynamic light scattering (DLS) and electrophoretic light scattering (ELS). The particles were illuminated by a dual 30 mW laser diode of 658 nm, producing time-dependent fluctuations in the intensity of laser light. The scattered light was collected at 165° for size measurements and 15° for Zeta-potential measurements.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of microarray chips for HPV detection
ce:section.ce:para: The SiNWs substrates were diced into 2.5 × 2.5 cm pieces to fabricate microarray chips. DNA probes containing an amino group and six linker carbon atoms at the 5′ end were diluted to a concentration of 50 μM and deployed on the functionalised substrates by contact printing (Cartesian Dispensing System, Genomic Solutions Ltd., UK) in a layout as illustrated in Fig. 2 .

The design of the chips included (Table 1 ) HPV 16-specific probes, HPV 16 probes co-immobilised with CDs-PDDA, and HPV 16 co-immobilised with CDs-PEI. Cy3-labelled control probes containing 5′-NH2 modifications were included to certify the attachment via covalent interaction with the epoxy groups from the SU-8 coating. Negative control probes ensured that the ssDNA obtained by aPCR hybridised specifically with the HPV 16 probes.

The biochips were incubated overnight at room temperature and subsequently washed with SSC + SDS, SSC, and deionised water to remove the unbound probes. The probe immobilisation was verified after incubation by scanning the fluorescence emitted at 532 nm by the Cy3 molecules attached at the 3′ end of the immobilisation control probes and stored at 4 °C until their use in hybridisation experiments.

Following Kaltenboeck et al. [48], we opted for a two-step aPCR in which the first step consists of a classical PCR, and the second step employs a single nested primer that generates ssDNA. Thus, to obtain ssDNA, we prepared two distinct PCR reactions, of which the first one was a classic PCR that generated the 217 bp amplicon, and the second one employed only one nested primer marked with a Cy5 molecule at the 5′ end. The PCR products of 217 bp were confirmed by electrophoresis in 2% agarose gel stained with SimplySafe (EURx, Poland). Table 2 shows the primers designed for classic PCR and asymmetric PCR.

The ssDNA was mixed with hybridisation buffer in a 1:1 ratio (for unpurified DNA) or at a final concentration of 0.1 µM ssDNA (for purified DNA). The mix was preheated to 60 °C, dispersed on the biochip surface, and enclosed with a coverslip to prevent solvent evaporation. The biochips were incubated in a humid chamber for 4 h at 42 °C. In the end, the unbound ssDNA was washed out with SSC + SDS, SSC, and deionised water. The results were read by scanning the fluorescence emitted at 532 nm (immobilisation controls) and 670 nm (hybridised samples).

The datasets generated with GenePix® Pro 7 were based on 768 technical replicates, of which the first set of 256 corresponds to HPV 16 without CDs, the second one corresponds to HPV 16 with CDs-PDDA and the last set of 256 replicates corresponds to HPV 16 with CDs-PEI. The average signal and local background intensities were processed and analysed in RStudio 1.4 [49] integrated development environment for R 4.1.1 release. The generated datasets were background-corrected and normalised by log10 transformation, as described in [14].

SEM images displayed in Fig. 3 reveal the conformal configuration of nanowires on the Si fragments ( Fig. 3 -a), full coverage with SU-8 (Fig. 3 -b), and the even distribution of CDs (Fig. 3 -c and Fig. 3 -d).

The in-plane SEM images show the conformal fabrication of high aspect ratio SiNWs through 1-MACE technique, which created a significant active area for the upcoming fluorescent experiments (Fig. 3 -a). The nanostructured layer is 8 μm thick, as measured in cross-section. The grouping of the SiNWs tips is explained by their bending tendency with the increase of their height [50], while the subsequent coating with SU-8 polymer reinforced the structures furthermore. After the 3-step thermal treatment of the SU-8 deposition, the polymer mass is fully embedding the SiNWs, as one may notice in the micrographs taken on the tilted and vertical specimens (Fig. 3 -b, c, d). Thus, the nanowires become solid-bonded into a polymer matrix that extends the active area by creating bridges between the cone-shaped structures and enables a stable platform for the covalent attachment of DNA [14]. The superficial layer of SU-8 promotes the covalent bonding of carbon dots and ssDNA probes by SN2-type II nucleophilic addition [14].

The covalent bonding also served as a mechanism for the adhesion of CDs from PDDA (Fig. 3 -c) and PEI (Fig. 3 -d) solutions onto the selected SiNWs/SU-8 fragments. The CDs deposited via PDDA showed a better distribution across the functionalised nanostructured substrates compared to the ones obtained from the PEI solution. In Fig. 3 -c, the carbon flakes were placed on top of the bundled SiNWs and deep under the surface alike, attached to the SU-8 embedding the pillars of SiNWs. The evidenced carbon flakes can be explained by the higher concentration of CDs and by non-covalent interactions between CDs-PDDA and the nanostructured surface coated with SU-8. In Fig. 3 -d, the CDs present in PEI were no longer seen on the SiNWs peaks but rather dispersed in the lower section of the SiNWs and to a lesser extent. Adding CDs on the SiNWs/SU-8 further increased the active area of the customised microarray platforms. It enhanced the fluorescent signal of the cyanine dye incorporated in the target DNA by asymmetric PCR, as demonstrated hereafter.
High-resolution TEM imaging disclosed the morpho-structural information about the CDs dispersed on the SiNWs/SU-8 heterostructure, as presented in Fig. 4 :

The lattice spacing of 0.21 nm and the reflection of the (100) facet of graphite proves the existence of CDs on SiNWs/SU-8 heterostructures [51–55]. The CDs-PDDA are widely spread (Fig. 4 -a), whereas the CDs-PEI (Fig. 4 -b) and are occasionally overlayed and can form CDs clusters [54,56]. The dynamic light scattering (DLS) measurements supported these findings by revealing a good polydispersity index for the PDDA-coated CDs and, respectively, two distribution domains for CDs-PEI, as it is revealed in Fig. 5 .

TEM complementary characterisation also provided additional information about Ag NPs on our biochips ( Fig. S2
,
Supporting Information ).

In the case of CDs coated with PDDA, a value of 914.7 ± 108.2 nm, was obtained, with an excellent polydispersity index – 0.207, while for the PEI-coated CDs, one can notice two distribution domains, with a mean diameter of 2194.9 ± 129.9 nm, as per the higher polydispersity index of 1.628. Next, the carbon dots were evaluated to assess their stability in terms of surface charge. For the CDs-PDDA, the measured surface charge was + 54.55 mV, while for amine-based dots - CDs-PEI, a higher potential value of + 77.49 mV was recorded. In both cases, there were positive values, due to the amine groups, showing thus successfully functionalisation and very good stability in both cases. Aditionally, the amine groups density on CDs-PDDA surface give a very good dispersity on SiNWs/SU-8 heterostructures surfaces, at it is revealed by TEM investigations.

Raman spectroscopy has been widely used to characterise the sp2 hybridised carbon atoms[57]. Moreover, the UV-Raman spectroscopy is more sensitive to the sp3 carbon linkages [58]. Since the PL depends on the surface state of the CDs [59], UV-Raman measurements with low penetration depth [60,61] were carried out.

The UV-Raman spectra in Fig. 6 of the SiNWs, SiNWs/SU-8 and CDs-PEI samples are dominated by the TO mode of the Si array at about 520 cm−1 [60], while 515 cm−1 is the main band for the CDs-PDDA sample (see Table S1-
Supporting Information ).

Silicon nanocrystal (Si nc)-containing SiOx was reported to give a Raman band at about 513 cm−1 [62]. Although UV excitation was used, a slight photoluminescence background was recorded for the SiNWs/SU-8 and CDs-PDDA, CDs-PEI samples. The photoluminescence background is highly dependent on the oxygen content of the Si included in the SiOx thin films (where x > 1.5) [63].

Although graphene and graphene oxide do not show fluorescence, quantum dots can trigger efficient fluorescence emission with biosensing applications [60]. Tunning of the emission can be achieved by modification of the spherical nanosized CDs and oxygen content (COOH and OH groups attached to the surface of the sp2 hybridised carbon atoms).

The annealed SU-8 photoresist spectrum (SiNWs/SU-8) in Fig. 6 shows spectral features of an sp2-carbon material, namely with the main bands (D and G bands) in the 1100–1800 cm−1 region. Analogous information about D and G bands for the CDs-PDDA, CDs-PEI samples was obtained by four-component fitting of the same region (Fig. 7 and Table S1 -
Supporting Information ).

The intensity ratio of the D and G bands, ID/IG, increasing in succession CDs-PEI (0.3243) < SiNWs/SU-8 (0.4043) < CDs-PDDA (0.47577) points out a more defective graphitic structure of the CDs-PDDA upon PDDA addition in contrast with the CDs-PEI sample. The latter sample encounters the decrease of surface defects by PEI addition. The AD”/AG area ratio has been reported [64] to be inversely proportional to PL intensity. Among the current cases, the SiNWs/SU-8 has the highest AD”/AG value, as depictable in Table S1
(
Supporting Information
), corresponding to the low fluorescence of the epoxy photoresist [65].

Obtaining the Raman quantitative data for the sp2-carbon materials uses empirical equations, which are rarely validated for the UV-excited samples. For instance, the equation proposed by Concado et al. [66] was applied to Raman data collected utilising the 325 nm laser. In this case, the CDs-PDDA sample showed the smallest in-plane correlation length (La) among the investigated samples ( Table S1
,
Supporting Information ).

XPS is an effective method to analyse the composition, chemical state, and molecular structure of CDs. The survey spectra from Fig. S3
(
Supporting Information
) revealed the most important features of the elemental surface composition. Fig. 8 presents the deconvolution of C1s spectra.

The deconvolution of high-resolution C1s spectra can be assigned to the following secondary species: π-π* satellite given by the peak located at 291.48 eV, which can be attributed to the aromatic structure of the exposed SU-8 (Fig. 8-b), C–C/C–H given by the peak located at 284.8 eV (Fig. 8-b-h) coming from the chemical structures overlayed on the microarray chip, C-O ones from the peak at 286.2 eV (Fig. 8b-h) which can be an indicator peak of the surface modification of SiNWs/SU-8 with DNA structures, while at 288–289.2 eV, the C = O species (Fig. 8c-h) were responsible for the registered peak. The difference between immobilization and hybridization relied on the modification of the C–C/C-O area ratio.
As can be noticed from the N1s spectra from Fig. 9 , there was an interaction between the ammonium and imine groups from the polymer structures.

There was a clear shift from the 401 eV position of N1s species implied in the specific groups for both PDDA and PEI, when ssDNA was immobilised, due to the modification of the electronic surrounding by physical interactions. The surface of hybridised samples has shown the characteristic features of the DNA purine and pyrimidine structures. The shift of imine N1s peak to 403 eV was caused by strong interface formation with PEI structures. PDDA most probably interacted with the functionalized SiNWs via electrostatic forces, as it is a strong polyelectrolyte.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Absorbance and photoluminescence study of SiNWs and CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The photoluminescence emission of the uncoated SiNWs (Fig. 10
) was studied at room temperature using the excitation wavelength of 300 nm generated by the xenon lamp (450 W).

SiNWs displayed a wide spectral band whose deconvolution indicated the existence of four Gaussian bands with emission maxima centred at 550 nm, 589 nm, 615 nm, and 645 nm. Dominant photoluminescence bands can be attributed to several phenomena, such as the degree of porosity of SiNWs [67] or the occurrence of quantum confinement effects (2D), whereas the low-intensity band centred at 645 nm can be generated by surface defects [68,69]. Other phenomena that could explain the photoluminescence of SiNWs are the defects at the SiOx / Si interface or the defect states at the oxide surface (Si – O bonds) and the presence of silicon nanocrystals [70]. In our case, the PL signal can be attributed to the porous nature of the nanowires, as revealed by TEM investigations (Fig. 4), and to the presence of the Si nanocrystals confirmed by Raman spectroscopy (Fig. 7).

The absorbance characteristic of SiNWs covers the entire visible spectrum due to their optimised geometry and size that demonstrated light scattering and trapping properties and greater efficiency for absorbing the incident radiation compared to bulk silicon [71,72].

Fig. 11 shows the optical absorption and the photoluminescence (PL) spectra of aqueous carbon dot solutions (CDs-PDDA and CDs-PEI), where PL emission was recorded for excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 − 540 nm.

The absorption spectra of CDs (Fig. 11 -a) display two distinct and prominent bands located at 200 and 275 nm for CDs-PEI and 225 and 285 nm for CDs-PDDA, which are assigned to π − π* transitions from the carbon double bonds backbone of the aromatic core. An absorption tail extending in the 300 – 550 nm region corresponds to the recorded luminescence and is assigned to σ − π and n − π* transitions from surface states induced by surface passivation with functional groups.

The band gap of the CDs was determined using the Tauc method (Fig. 11 - a - inset) and is consistent with the previous studies [44,73]. The results showed that the optical band gap value of 2.94 eV for CDs-PEI was decreased to 2.88 eV for CDs-PDDA, which could be driven by a slightly increased number of NH2 groups [74].

The surface passivation with organic and polymeric species has led to the increase and displacement of the optical response. Besides the significant PL bands centered at ∼ 430 nm for CDs-PDDA (Fig. 11 – b) and at ∼ 500 nm for CDs-PEI (Fig. 11 – c), the PDDA functionalisation demonstrated more intense and broad emission bands towards the red wavelength region. This behaviour was related to the redistribution of charges resulting from functionalisation: NH2 was positively charged by donating the electron from the free electron pair of NH2 to the anti-bond state in the benzene ring resulting in a decrease in the band gap energy. One can remark that at 320 nm excitation (wavelength used in Raman studies), the intensity of the PL signal of CDs-PEI is about 5 times higher than in the case CDs-PDDA, as per the decreased value calculated for Raman AD”/AG ratio.

The nanostructured biochip used the Cy5 fluorophore as a red marker for hybridisation detection. Cy5 was photoexcited with a 635 nm laser paired with a 670/30 nm bandpass filter and had a maximum emission at 670 nm (Fig. 9 - d). Notably, the CDs-PDDA and CDs-PEI were non-emissive at the working wavelength of the biosensor (635 nm), but their presence favoured a non-radiative energy transfer to the chromophore [75].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microarray results on the nanostructured platforms
ce:section.ce:para:
The hybridisation on the microarray chips was done with labelled ssDNA corresponding to HPV 16. The labelled ssDNA was obtained by classical PCR followed by asymmetric PCR with a nested primer (primers presented in Table 2). The PCR amplification products obtained by classical PCR were visualised by agarose gel electrophoresis ( Figure S4
,
Supporting Information ). The labelled ssDNA sample was dispersed on biochips on which specific probes define the following areas: negative controls – (−) Controls - (128 replicates), immobilisation controls – (+) Controls (128 replicates) and 256 technical replicates per assessment (256 replicates for HPV 16 probe – H16, 256 replicates for HPV 16 co-immobilised with CDs-PDDA – H16/CDs-PDDA and 256 replicates for HPV 16 co-immobilised with CDs-PEI – H16/CDs-PEI). The layout of the arrays is presented in Fig. 2 for a clear overview of the biochip fabrication.
Fig. 12 shows the fluorescent results of the hybridisation with purified and unpurified amplicons.

The spot morphology was uniform in the depicted microarrays, with fluorescent hybridisation intensities that appeared typically consistent from one replicate to another. A few exceptions occurred from the uniformity of the SU-8 layer, and they were removed as outliers from the forthcoming graphical and statistical analyses. The high intensity of the immobilisation control marked a proper bonding of the DNA probes on the SU-8-coated SiNWs. The arrays corresponding to the negative controls showed a very dim fluorescence. We attributed this observation to the photoluminescent emission of the nanowires at a maximum of 645 nm (Fig. 10), similar to the emission response of the Cy5 dye (Fig. 11 - d). The remarked photoluminescence was localised only at the array level, as the pins employed for probe deposition have notched the SU-8 layer. To validate this observation, we added in Fig. 12 a scan of the control samples (last row) at an excitation wavelength of 635 nm, corresponding to Cy5 dye. The imprints of the pins were also present for the positive controls. Moreover, we repeated the experiment on a Si support coated with SU-8 ( Fig. S5 , Supporting information ) to certify that the hybridisation reaction is specific. As expected, the flat support did not show fluorescence at 635 nm in the arrays corresponding to the positive and negative controls. In future work, we will exploit the photoluminescent properties of SiNWs for the label-free optical detection [19] of DNA.

Regardless of using purified or unpurified amplicons, higher hybridisation intensities were observed for the HPV 16 probes co-immobilised with the two types of CDs. To quantify the enhancement of the fluorescent signal provided by the CDs, graphical and statistical data analysis was performed on the hybridisation results. The graphical representations (Fig. 13 ), correlated with the microarray images from Fig. 12 show higher signal intensities for the biochips hybridised with ssDNA that did not include a purification step after the PCR reaction.

We expected a higher fluorescent signal for the purified samples since the unpurified amplicons also contain nucleotides that might impede the efficacy of the hybridisation process. Purification highlighted improvements in the standard deviation (average σ of 0.11 for purified amplicon versus an average σ of 0.2 for unpurified amplicon) and variation coefficients (average CV 3.24 % versus an average CV of 5.18 % for unpurified amplicon). However, the results showed lower fluorescent signals exhibited by the purified sample. Further analysis of the signal-to-noise ratio was conducted, as it is a performance feature of the microarray substrates representing the ability to discriminate the signal coming from the spot from the surrounding background noise [76]. Here, the ratio of the signal-to-both-standard deviation (SSDR) [77], with a threshold value of 0.80, was applied to compare the level of fluorescent signal related to the hybridisation results with both purified and unpurified reaction products. A value of 3.536 was obtained for the microarray containing unpurified reaction product, which is about 2 times higher than the value obtained in a prior study on SiNWs (1.949), and ∼ 3 times higher compared to a commercial microarray glass support (1.247) [14]. However, the hybridisation with the purified reaction product led to an SSDR value of 0.295, below the threshold value of 0.80. This might be due to the loss of ssDNA during the amplicon purification step. We also considered the possibility that a higher fluorescent signal for the unpurified samples might arise from unspecific hybridisation. Though the fluorescent Cy5 molecule is attached only to the primer, and the primer sequence does not present complementarity to the hybridisation probe, it is improbable that the higher signal is from a lack of hybridisation specificity. Unspecific amplification from the labelled primer is also unlikely due to the nesting of the primer on the amplicon resulting in the first PCR step. This result favours the omission of the purification step, which reduces the time of the assay. Thus, the highest signal intensities (3.744, 4.303, 3.959) were observed after hybridisation with unpurified amplicons.

By analysing the effects of CDs on the hybridisation parameters, we revealed that the carbon dots functionalised with CDs-PDDA enhance the signal intensity (4.303), as well as the standard deviation (0.13) and variation coefficient (3.12 %). The CDs functionalised with PDDA and PEI promoted an increase in the fluorescence of the Cy5 dye attached to the ssDNA generated by asymmetric PCR, leading to an enhanced signal-to-noise ratio of the biochip. A possible explanation resides in the optical and physicochemical properties of CDs, which acted as long-range energy transfer donors for dye-labelled ss DNA, further improving the performance and efficiency of detection [78].

In this work, we report for the first time the use of CDs to increase DNA microarray biochips' detection performance. Our prototypes involved the use of biochips on SiNWs/SU-8 substrates, to which CDs in PDDA or PEI solutions were attached. To ensure the reliability of our prototypes, we conducted thorough investigations using complementary morpho-structural, Raman, XPS, UV–Vis, and microarray techniques. The data consistency obtained through microarray investigations revealed outstanding statistical significance.

We have successfully developed a large active area for biomolecular interactions through the use of a 3D support composed of high aspect ratio SiNWs, manufactured using the 1-MACE technique. The support was coated with SU-8, allowing for covalent bonding of DNA probes and resulting in a fluorescent hybridization intensity of 3.744 for HPV 16. This achieved a standard deviation of 0.26 and a variation coefficient of 7.13%, indicating a high level of precision in our methodology.

The SU-8 layer allowed also the bonding of CDs (diam. 5 – 10 nm) functionalised with PDDA and PEI. The CD enhancement on the fluorescent intensity of HPV16 was owing to a non-radiative energy transfer to the Cy5 chromophore attached to the ssDNA generated by asymmetric PCR. CDs-PDDA provided the best results, increasing the hybridisation signal intensity of HPV 16 to 4.313 while obtaining a better standard deviation (0.12) and variation coefficient (3.12%). The enhancement provided by CDs-PDDA was favoured by the higher concentration of the solution and the non-covalent interactions.

A promising perspective lies in adapting the described microarray biochip for solid-phase isothermal amplification. Techniques like LAMP or RPA, which are isothermal amplification methods, offer a straightforward approach to viral nucleic acid detection, by achieving amplification, labelling and hybridisation in one single step while eliminating the need to obtain ssDNA. Moreover, with careful primer design and the use of a reverse transcriptase, this method can be extended to both RNA and DNA targets, making for a promising mutliplex detection platform with improved fluorescent readout.
